content.full_text:
March 13, 1980


Mr. George Murchison
Spravy for Senate Committee
P.O. Box 15207
Long Beach, CA 90815

Dear Mr. Murchison:

Thank you for your letter of February 26, 1980, in which you inquire about the propriety under the Political Reform Act of a campaign management arrangement.

As I understand it you will have three separate campaigns, two state and one federal, run by the same campaign management firm. The firm will pay all bills for the joint headquarters and staff, but each campaign will have its own committee which will raise money for its own candidate only and pay the firm a fee to cover the shared expenses on an agreed-in-advance proportional basis.

The proposal as you state it implies issues concerning at least two sections of the Political Reform Act. The Act defines as a "contribution" any payment except to the extent that the full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes (See Govt. Code §82115 and 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18215).

You propose to allocate the costs of the joint headquarters and staff on a fixed percentage basis - 37% each for two candidates and 26% for the third. If this allocation bears a fair and reasonable relationship to the benefit and use derived by each candidate from the joint expenses, then there will be consideration for the payment made by each candidate, and none of the candidates will be making contributions to any of the others. If on the other hand any of the candidates is paying more than his or her campaign reasonably receives in use and benefit from the joint expenses, the excess will constitute a contribution to the candidate or candidates paying less than the benefit received. Such a contribution would have to be designated on the payments schedules of the donor's reports and reported as a non-monetary contribution received by the recipient.

The other area of the Political Reform Act implicated by your proposal concerns the payment of expenses by the campaign management firm. Govt. Code §84303 contains the following language:

Mr. George Murchison                    -2-                    March 13, 1980


     No expenditure shall be made, other than overhead
or normal operating expenses, by an agent or independent
contractor, including but not limited to an advertising
agency, on behalf of, or for the benefit of any candidate
or committee unless it is reported by the candidate or
committee as if the expenditure were made directly by
the candidate or committee, unless the agent or inde-
pendent contractor files a campaign statement reporting
the expenditure. The agent or independent contractor
shall make known to the candidate or committee all in-
formation required to be reported by this section.

     As you describe your proposal, Crow and Campbell will be making
expenditures as agent or independent contractor for each of the
three candidates. Each state candidate, therefore, will need to
report his or her payments to Crow and Campbell.

     In addition, unless Crow and Campbell itself files reports as a
recipient committee, each candidate will also be required to report
the expenditures which Crow and Campbell make for him or her, as if
the expenditures were made directly by the candidate. Expenditures
made over by Crow and Campbell to vendors of goods and services not
amounting to $100 for any one vendor may simply be lump-summed and
reported as total expenditures over of under $100 each (See Govt. Code
§84210(e)). For any vendor of goods and services to whom the ex-
penditure over for a particular candidate is $100 or more the vendor
must be identified, in the report, by full name and street address,
amount of each separate expenditure to each such person during the
period covered by the campaign statement, and description of the
consideration for which the expenditure was made (See Govt. Code
§84210(i)).

     Crow and Campbell are required as agents or independent con-
tractors to supply to the candidates all information needed by the
candidates to comply with Govt. Code §84303 as quoted above.

     It should be noted that these requirements apply to the proposal
you present only insofar as the latter involves or affects candidates
for local, state or statewide office. To the extent the proposal in-
volves or affects candidates for federal office, it may be governed by
federal law with respect to which we do not advise. You may contact
the Federal Election Commission toll-free at 800-424-9530 for infor-
mation about federal requirements.

     While this response is not an exclusive catalogue of the require-
ments of the California Political Reform Act applicable to your proposal,
I hope it is helpful for your purposes. If you have additional ques-
tions about the Act, you may call me at 916-322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        John Giansallo
